title: Eosinophilia Workup
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Hematology
objectives: Define eosinophilia severity levels (mild, moderate, severe)
Develop a systematic diagnostic approach to unexplained eosinophilia
Recognize hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) and its variants
Identify FIP1L1-PDGFRA rearrangement and its treatment implications

guidelineReferences.name: WHO/ICC 2024 Eosinophilic Disorders Update
guidelineReferences.citation: Gotlib J, et al. World Health Organization and International Consensus Classification of eosinophilic disorders: 2024 update. Am J Hematol. 2024
keyPoints: Corticosteroids first-line for HES and lymphocytic variant
Exclude secondary causes before primary workup

guidelineReferences.name: British Guidelines for Eosinophilia Investigation
guidelineReferences.citation: Shomali W, et al. Br J Haematol. 2017;176:553-572

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old man is referred for evaluation of persistent eosinophilia discovered incidentally. He reports mild fatigue and occasional itchy skin but is otherwise asymptomatic. He has no known allergies, has not traveled internationally, and takes no medications. He denies cough, diarrhea, or weight loss.

Past Medical History: None significant

Medications: None

Allergies: NKDA

Social History: No travel, no pets, works as an accountant

physicalExam.skin: Faint scattered excoriations, no rash, no nodules
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, no murmurs, no edema

labs.Peripheral Smear: Eosinophilia confirmed, no blasts, no dysplasia

questions.stem: This patient has an absolute eosinophil count (AEC) of 3,200/μL.
questions.question: How is eosinophilia defined and classified by severity?

options.text: HES can be diagnosed with any eosinophil count
Severity classification is not clinically useful

EOSINOPHILIA: AEC >500/μL; MILD: 500-1,500/μL; MODERATE: 1,500-5,000/μL; SEVERE: >5,000/μL; HYPEREOSINOPHILIA: >1,500/μL on 2 occasions >1 month apart; HES: Hypereosinophilia + end-organ damage + exclusion of other causes

questions.explanation: Eosinophilia classification: Normal AEC <500/μL. MILD: 500-1,500/μL. MODERATE: 1,500-5,000/μL (this patient at 3,200). SEVERE: >5,000/μL. HYPEREOSINOPHILIA: AEC >1,500/μL documented on 2 occasions >1 month apart. HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROME (HES): Hypereosinophilia + evidence of end-organ damage + exclusion of secondary and other primary causes. Idiopathic hypereosinophilia = hypereosinophilia without organ damage and unknown etiology.

whyChain.answer: Higher eosinophil counts correlate with organ damage risk, especially cardiac (endomyocardial fibrosis). Severe eosinophilia (>5,000) warrants urgent workup. Moderate eosinophilia (1,500-5,000) requires systematic evaluation for secondary and primary causes.

questions.stem: You are systematically evaluating for secondary causes of eosinophilia.
questions.question: What are the common secondary (reactive) causes of eosinophilia?

options.text: INFECTIONS: Parasites (helminths most common globally), fungi; ALLERGIC/ATOPIC: Asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema, food allergy; DRUGS: Many medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs, anticonvulsants); AUTOIMMUNE: EGPA, IBD, connective tissue diseases; MALIGNANCY: Solid tumors, lymphoma (reactive); ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

questions.explanation: Secondary (reactive) eosinophilia causes >90% of cases. Key categories: (1) PARASITES: Helminth infections (tissue-invasive: Strongyloides, Toxocara, Ascaris, hookworm, Trichinella) - #1 cause globally; (2) ALLERGIC/ATOPIC: Asthma, rhinitis, eczema, food allergy - usually mild eosinophilia; (3) DRUGS: Antibiotics (penicillins, sulfa), NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, allopurinol - consider DRESS; (4) AUTOIMMUNE: EGPA (Churg-Strauss), IBD (especially Crohn's), IgG4-related disease; (5) MALIGNANCY: Hodgkin lymphoma, some solid tumors; (6) Adrenal insufficiency (loss of cortisol suppression).

whyChain.answer: Helminth parasites are the most common cause of marked eosinophilia worldwide. Even without travel history, consider Strongyloides (can persist decades, endemic in US Southeast). Check stool O&P x3 (single sample misses many infections). Serology for Strongyloides, Toxocara if high suspicion.
Normal IgE suggests no obvious allergic driver. Stool O&P negative x3. No medications. No travel. No atopic symptoms. Secondary causes largely excluded - need to consider primary causes.

sources.text: StatPearls: Eosinophilia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Secondary causes have been excluded. You are now evaluating for clonal/primary eosinophilia.
questions.question: What are the primary (clonal) causes of eosinophilia?

options.text: MYELOID NEOPLASMS WITH EOSINOPHILIA: FIP1L1-PDGFRA (responds to imatinib), PDGFRB rearrangements, FGFR1 rearrangements, PCM1-JAK2; CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC LEUKEMIA (CEL); SYSTEMIC MASTOCYTOSIS with eosinophilia; Other myeloid neoplasms (CML, MDS, AML M4Eo)
Bone marrow evaluation is never needed

questions.explanation: Primary (clonal) eosinophilia: (1) MLN with tyrosine kinase fusions: FIP1L1-PDGFRA (most common, exquisitely imatinib-sensitive), PDGFRB, FGFR1, PCM1-JAK2; (2) Chronic eosinophilic leukemia (CEL): Clonal without above rearrangements; (3) Systemic mastocytosis with eosinophilia (KIT D816V mutation); (4) Other myeloid neoplasms: CML, AML (especially M4Eo with CBF translocations), MDS, MPN. FIP1L1-PDGFRA testing critical - transforms treatment with imatinib.

whyChain.answer: FIP1L1-PDGFRA is present in 10-20% of clonal HES. It's a cryptic deletion on chromosome 4 creating a constitutively active tyrosine kinase. EXQUISITELY responds to LOW-DOSE IMATINIB (100mg daily). Without treatment: cardiac damage, progression to leukemia. With imatinib: durable remissions, often complete molecular response.

Test for FIP1L1-PDGFRA by FISH or RT-PCR (can be done on peripheral blood). Check serum tryptase (elevated in mastocytosis and some PDGFRA+ cases). If negative but persistent unexplained hypereosinophilia, bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics, flow cytometry, and molecular testing.

sources.text: StatPearls: Eosinophilia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are classifying types of hypereosinophilic syndrome.
questions.question: What are the major variants of HES?

options.text: All HES is the same entity

MYELOPROLIFERATIVE HES: Clonal (PDGFRA, etc.), male predominant, elevated tryptase, cardiac involvement, responds to imatinib; LYMPHOCYTIC VARIANT: Aberrant T-cell clone producing IL-5, skin involvement, responds to steroids; IDIOPATHIC HES: Diagnosis of exclusion when other causes ruled out

questions.explanation: HES variants: (1) MYELOPROLIFERATIVE VARIANT: Clonal myeloid disorder (FIP1L1-PDGFRA most common), male predominant (9:1), elevated B12 and tryptase, high risk of cardiac damage (endomyocardial fibrosis), responds to imatinib; (2) LYMPHOCYTIC VARIANT: Aberrant T-cell clone (CD3-CD4+ or other phenotype) produces IL-5 driving eosinophilia, skin involvement common, steroid-responsive, risk of T-cell lymphoma progression; (3) IDIOPATHIC HES: Diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out secondary, clonal, and lymphocytic causes; steroid-responsive.

whyChain.answer: Myeloproliferative features: Male, splenomegaly, elevated B12/tryptase, anemia, abnormal smear. Lymphocytic features: Elevated IgE, skin involvement, abnormal T-cell phenotype on flow cytometry. Calculate AEC/tryptase ratio: <100 suggests mastocytosis, >100 suggests HES.

questions.stem: You are screening for end-organ damage from eosinophilia.
questions.question: What organs are affected by eosinophilic infiltration and how should they be screened?

options.text: CARDIAC (most serious): Endomyocardial fibrosis, restrictive cardiomyopathy, intracardiac thrombus - screen with troponin, echo, cardiac MRI; PULMONARY: Infiltrates, fibrosis; GI: Eosinophilic esophagitis/gastroenteritis; SKIN: Urticaria, angioedema, dermatitis; NEURO: Neuropathy, CNS involvement

questions.explanation: Eosinophil-mediated organ damage: (1) CARDIAC (most critical): Endomyocardial fibrosis (Löffler endocarditis), restrictive cardiomyopathy, mural thrombus, valvular disease. Screen: troponin, BNP, echocardiogram, cardiac MRI. Especially common in myeloproliferative HES; (2) PULMONARY: Infiltrates, fibrosis, pleural effusion; (3) GI: EoE, eosinophilic gastroenteritis; (4) SKIN: Urticaria, angioedema, dermatitis, pruritus; (5) NEURO: Peripheral neuropathy, encephalopathy, stroke (from cardiac emboli); (6) HEMATOLOGIC: Thrombosis risk.

whyChain.why: Why is cardiac involvement so serious?
whyChain.answer: Eosinophil granule proteins (MBP, ECP) directly damage myocardium. Progression: necrosis → thrombosis → fibrosis. Can lead to restrictive cardiomyopathy, heart failure, arrhythmias, embolic stroke. Irreversible if untreated. Screen ALL patients with hypereosinophilia with troponin and echo.

questions.stem: You are planning treatment based on the type of eosinophilia identified.
questions.question: How is hypereosinophilia/HES treated?

options.text: FIP1L1-PDGFRA positive: IMATINIB (low dose 100mg, dramatic response); PDGFRB: Imatinib; FGFR1: Pemigatinib; Lymphocytic/Idiopathic HES: CORTICOSTEROIDS first-line (prednisone 1mg/kg); Refractory: Hydroxyurea, interferon-α, mepolizumab (anti-IL-5)

questions.explanation: Treatment by HES type: (1) FIP1L1-PDGFRA positive: IMATINIB 100mg daily - dramatic, durable responses, often complete molecular remission; (2) Other PDGFR rearrangements: Imatinib; (3) FGFR1 rearrangement: Pemigatinib (FDA approved); (4) Lymphocytic variant HES: CORTICOSTEROIDS (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg); (5) Idiopathic HES: Corticosteroids first-line; (6) Steroid-refractory: Hydroxyurea, interferon-α, mepolizumab (anti-IL-5), benralizumab; (7) Emergent treatment for severe organ damage: High-dose steroids ± imatinib.

whyChain.why: Why steroids before imatinib workup complete?
whyChain.answer: In severe HES with organ damage, start steroids immediately while awaiting molecular results. BUT: Give steroids BEFORE or WITH imatinib in PDGFRA+ patients - sudden eosinophil death from imatinib can cause acute cardiac decompensation. Steroids provide protection.

sources.text: StatPearls: Eosinophilia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient has eosinophilia with asthma and neuropathy.
questions.question: How do you differentiate HES from eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA/Churg-Strauss)?

options.text: EGPA features: Asthma (usually severe, late-onset), sinusitis, peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex), ANCA positive in ~40% (MPO-ANCA), histologic VASCULITIS, elevated CRP; HES: No vasculitis, ANCA negative, lower CRP; Treatment overlap: Both respond to steroids and mepolizumab
ANCA is always positive in EGPA

questions.explanation: HES vs EGPA differentiation: EGPA features: (1) Asthma (severe, often late-onset); (2) Chronic rhinosinusitis/nasal polyps; (3) Peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex classic); (4) ANCA positive ~40% (usually MPO/p-ANCA); (5) Histologic vasculitis (necrotizing, granulomatous); (6) Higher CRP (>40 mg/L). HES features: No vasculitis, ANCA negative, lower CRP. Overlap: Both have eosinophilia, both can have cardiac/pulmonary involvement, both respond to steroids. Anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab) approved for both.

whyChain.answer: EGPA is a systemic vasculitis requiring immunosuppression (steroids ± cyclophosphamide/rituximab). HES treatment varies by subtype (imatinib for PDGFRA+, steroids for idiopathic). Overlap exists and distinction can be challenging - some consider EGPA within HES spectrum.

sources.text: StatPearls: Eosinophilia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with moderate eosinophilia.
questions.question: What is the systematic workup for this patient?

options.text: Step 1: Exclude secondary causes (parasites - already done, drugs, allergies, autoimmune); Step 2: Screen for organ damage (troponin, echo, CXR, LFTs); Step 3: Test FIP1L1-PDGFRA by FISH/PCR; Step 4: If negative, bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics, flow, molecular; Step 5: If all negative = idiopathic hypereosinophilia; monitor vs treat based on symptoms/organ involvement

questions.explanation: Systematic eosinophilia workup: (1) EXCLUDE SECONDARY: Stool O&P x3 ✓, medication review ✓, IgE/allergy evaluation ✓, autoimmune workup if indicated; (2) SCREEN FOR ORGAN DAMAGE: Troponin, BNP, echocardiogram (cardiac), CXR, PFTs (pulmonary), LFTs; (3) PRIMARY WORKUP: FIP1L1-PDGFRA by FISH or RT-PCR (peripheral blood), serum tryptase, B12; (4) BONE MARROW if persistent unexplained hypereosinophilia: Morphology, cytogenetics, flow cytometry (T-cell phenotype), molecular panel; (5) DIAGNOSIS: Idiopathic hypereosinophilia (no organ damage) vs Idiopathic HES (with organ damage) if all testing negative. Treat based on organ involvement and subtype.

whyChain.answer: Moderate eosinophilia (3,200), asymptomatic except mild pruritus. Secondary causes largely excluded. Next: (1) Check troponin/BNP, echocardiogram to rule out cardiac involvement; (2) Send FIP1L1-PDGFRA; (3) If negative and no organ damage, can observe with serial monitoring. If organ damage found, bone marrow and treatment indicated.

sources.text: StatPearls: Eosinophilia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
